meta.title: Mercedes A200 CDI Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes‑Benz A200 CDI engine — OM640 and OM651. W169 and W176 generations, real UK costs and reliability. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

@graph.name: Mercedes‑Benz A200 CDI Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz A200 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM640 engine typically costs £1,500–£3,500 supply only; the OM651 costs £2,500–£6,500. Fitting adds £800–£1,400. Timing chain replacement on the OM651 can cost £1,500–£3,500 if caught early. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz A200 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the generation. The OM640 (W169, 2004–2012) is rated ★★★☆☆ — a robust cast‑iron block diesel with injector and EGR issues as the main weaknesses. The OM651 (W176, 2012–2018) is rated ★★½☆☆ — refined but problematic, with timing chain stretch, injector failure, and EGR/DPF issues all well‑documented.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz A200 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM651 timing chain stretch (the most critical — can destroy the engine), injector leak‑off pipe failure (diesel smell/pooling), injector failure, EGR valve clogging, and DPF issues. The OM640 is generally more reliable than the OM651.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑Benz A200 CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. Against a well‑kept W176 A200 CDI worth £4,000–£8,000, a £3,500 recon is a significant investment — assess carefully. For a W169 with the OM640, the engine is generally more robust and replacement is often worthwhile if the body is solid.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz A200 CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The A200 CDI uses OM640.941 (W169, 2004–2012) or OM651.930 (W176, 2012–2018). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM651's timing chain, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A timing chain and tensioner replacement (£1,500–£3,500) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain snap (£2,500–£6,500 engine replacement). At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, book an inspection immediately. The chain is located at the rear of the engine, so it's a labour‑intensive job, but it's far cheaper than a new engine.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz A-Class A200 CDI Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an A200 CDI engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Timing chain replacement on the OM651 is a labour‑intensive job requiring gearbox removal.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz A200 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: If caught early; chain at rear makes it labour‑intensive but repairable
Replace leak‑off pipes; don't ignore diesel smell
Replace failed injectors; use updated parts
Regular cleaning; use quality fuel and motorway runs to prevent
Preventative — regular motorway runs are the best prevention
Less common than OM651, but repairable if caught early

buyingChecks.title: Buying This A200 CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with documented oil changes
* Evidence of timing chain work on OM651‑engined cars (W176)
* W169 with the OM640 — generally the more robust engine
* Good service history — the OM651 is unforgiving of neglect
avoidIf: * Cold‑start rattle with no timing chain history — this is the single most important check on W176
* Diesel smell from the engine bay — potential injector leak
* DPF warning lights or regeneration issues with no diagnosis
* Poor service history — the OM651 is sensitive to long oil change intervals

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The A200 CDI badge spans two very different engines. The W169's OM640 is the more robust of the two — a cast‑iron block diesel that's reliable when maintained. The W176's OM651 is a refined but troublesome engine — timing chain stretch, injector failure, and EGR/DPF issues are all well‑documented. Buy the OM640 if you want reliability; buy the OM651 only with documented chain history.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz A200 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling noise on startup (timing chain warning on OM651 — the most critical check)
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM640 for W169 2004–2012, OM651 for W176 2012–2018) — see the Era Map above
* Diesel Smell — Any strong diesel smell from the engine bay? Injector leak‑off pipe or injector failure indicator
* Injector Pooling — Any diesel pooling near the injectors? Leak‑off pipe failure
* DPF Status — Any DPF warning lights or regeneration issues?
* EGR System — Any loss of power or black smoke? EGR clogging indicator
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved timing, injector, or emissions issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures; often point to EGR, DPF, or injector problems
